title: SEGMENT 13 — Adenoviral vaccines during COVID: Sputnik V, J&J/Janssen, AstraZeneca/Oxford, and the lessons learned

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Adenoviral vaccines during COVID: Sputnik V, J&J/Janssen, AstraZeneca/Oxford, and the lessons learned
Let's stitch together the COVID-era story, because it is the moment adenoviral vectors went from a niche tool to a global public health technology, and it deserves to be told as a single arc.
When SARS-CoV-2 emerged in late 2019 and early 2020, the scientific community had a remarkably broad menu of vaccine platforms to draw on.
mRNA vaccines, which had been in development for over a decade but had never been licensed before, would famously become two of the dominant products — Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna.
Inactivated whole-virus vaccines, the classical platform, would be deployed by several Chinese and Indian manufacturers.
And adenoviral vector vaccines would constitute a major share of doses delivered worldwide, particularly outside the wealthiest countries.
The major adenoviral COVID vaccines, in roughly the order they entered widespread deployment, were:
Sputnik V — developed by the Gamaleya Institute in Russia.
It uses a heterologous prime-boost: first dose is Ad26-vectored spike, second dose is Ad5-vectored spike.
The two serotypes are antigenically distinct enough that anti-Ad26 antibodies from dose one don't substantially neutralize the Ad5 in dose two.
Sputnik V was widely deployed in Russia, parts of Latin America, parts of the Middle East, and parts of Asia.
The Johnson and Johnson / Janssen vaccine — developed by Janssen Pharmaceutical, a J&J subsidiary in the Netherlands.
Single dose, Ad26-vectored spike.
The original Phase III trial demonstrated meaningful efficacy with one shot, which was an enormous logistical advantage compared to two-dose regimens in resource-limited settings.
AstraZeneca-Oxford / Vaxzevria / Covishield — developed at Oxford and manufactured at scale by AstraZeneca and, importantly, by the Serum Institute of India under the Covishield name.
ChAdOx1-vectored spike.
The Serum Institute supplied vast quantities to India and to COVAX, the WHO-led global vaccine equity initiative.
AstraZeneca/Covishield was the single most widely deployed COVID vaccine in many low and middle income countries.
CanSino Convidecia — developed in China, Ad5-vectored spike.
Used in China and in several countries that received it through bilateral arrangements.
A few patterns emerge from the COVID adenoviral vaccine experience that are worth pulling out.
Pattern one: adenoviral vaccines were generally cheaper, more stable, and easier to distribute than mRNA vaccines.
AstraZeneca specifically did not require ultra-cold storage; standard refrigeration would do.

This made adenoviral vaccines especially well-suited to deployment in countries without robust cold-chain infrastructure, and it is one of the main reasons adenoviral vaccines were so important in the global response, particularly in 2021 before mRNA supply scaled up.
Pattern two: adenoviral vaccines produced strong cellular immune responses, sometimes stronger than mRNA vaccines.
The encoded spike is expressed inside transduced cells and is partially processed and presented on MHC class I, eliciting CD8 T cell responses.
mRNA vaccines also produce cellular responses, but the relative balance of T cell versus antibody response differs between platforms.

For long-term protection against severe disease, a strong T cell response is valuable, because T cells can recognize multiple peptide fragments of the spike even when the surface antibody-binding sites have mutated.
This contributed to the durability of adenoviral vaccine protection against severe disease through successive variants.
Pattern three: adenoviral vaccines showed reduced efficacy against early variants compared to mRNA vaccines, but reasonable protection against severe outcomes.

Head-to-head efficacy comparisons against symptomatic infection generally favored mRNA platforms, but for the most important clinical outcome — preventing hospitalization and death — adenoviral vaccines performed well.
Pattern four: pre-existing immunity to the vector did affect responses, especially for the Ad5-based vaccines.
Studies of CanSino in populations with high Ad5 seroprevalence showed reduced antibody responses compared to populations with lower Ad5 seroprevalence.
This was a vindication of decades of warnings from the vector community about the seroprevalence problem.
Pattern five: the VITT side effect, discussed in the previous segment, was a real and serious phenomenon — rare, but real.
It changed risk-benefit calculations for younger patients with access to alternative vaccines, and led to use restrictions for AstraZeneca and J&J in several countries by mid-2021.
The mechanism is still being studied.
It appears specific to the adenoviral platform, not to the spike antigen itself; mRNA vaccines did not produce VITT.
What were the broader lessons?
Several, and they're going to shape the next decade of adenoviral vaccine development.
Lesson one: adenoviral vectors are deployable at vast scale.
Hundreds of millions of doses, with manufacturing distributed across multiple continents, demonstrated that the platform can be scaled up urgently for pandemic response.
The cell culture infrastructure, the purification workflows, the fill-finish capacity all proved adequate to global emergency demand.
Lesson two: rare and chimpanzee serotypes are the way forward.
Ad26 and ChAdOx1 outperformed Ad5 in populations with high Ad5 seroprevalence, exactly as the vector community had predicted.
Future adenoviral vaccines will overwhelmingly use rare or non-human serotypes for primary doses.
Lesson three: heterologous prime-boost is powerful.
Sputnik V's two-serotype regimen, and later mix-and-match studies with adenoviral prime followed by mRNA boost, consistently showed strong immune responses.
The future of vaccination, particularly for pathogens that require sustained immunity, may involve deliberate platform mixing across doses.
Lesson four: even rare serious side effects can shape deployment.
VITT was rare enough that the overall risk-benefit ratio of adenoviral COVID vaccines clearly favored vaccination during the acute pandemic.
But understanding rare adverse events better, ideally before deployment rather than after, is a critical area for future development.
Better preclinical models for VITT-like syndromes are an active research priority.
Lesson five: adenoviral vectors are a real, durable part of the vaccine toolkit going forward.
They were not a stopgap that mRNA technology displaced.

They have unique advantages — strong cellular immunity, room-temperature stability, cheaper production — that make them the right choice for some indications and some settings even when mRNA is available.
The next decade will likely see adenoviral vaccines for pathogens including influenza, RSV, HIV, malaria, tuberculosis, Lassa, Marburg, and Ebola, alongside the continued evolution of mRNA platforms.
That's the COVID story.

The same property — immune visibility — that killed Jesse Gelsinger in 1999 was deliberately exploited at planetary scale in 2020 and 2021 to teach hundreds of millions of immune systems about SARS-CoV-2 spike.
The truck didn't change.
The use case changed.
And in changing the use case, the field finally found a job where the truck's noisiness was exactly what was wanted.
